name: session-start
description: Agent is launched in a fresh cwd, writes its first transcript line, sits idle. The recording proves irrlicht binds the session to ready + a PID within ~1s of agent launch. Adapter-specific note: claudecode does not create the per-session transcript file until the first user input lands — the recipe sends a 1-token nudge ('hi') so the transcript materializes and the UUID-keyed session row appears. The pre-session row (proc-<PID>) is observable from launch independent of the nudge.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's two Expected bullets are (1) the session appears in `ready` within 1s of agent launch and (2) the PID is bound within 1s. Both are directly observable for the aider adapter. Aider writes `.aider.chat.history.md` (a per-cwd markdown chat log) on launch; the daemon's FilesUnderCWD watcher emits `transcript_new` immediately and the classifier lands the session in `ready` inside the first scanner tick. The proc-<PID> pre-session row (~50 ms in the recording) covers any gap.

PID binding happens at the same tick via `pid_discovered`. The recording shows process_exited within 250 ms of birth — aider's headless `--print` mode is extremely short-lived — but session_birth and pid_bind both fire within the spec's 1000 ms budget, well before exit.
caveats: Aider's transcript is markdown, not JSONL; assertions about JSONL line counts (used elsewhere) don't apply. Aider also exits very quickly in --print mode (~200ms), so any post-recording assertions about an idle ready window need to anchor before process_exited.
Headless aider may briefly show the working state if the daemon catches the in-flight LLM call, but for the session-start spec only ready and pid_discovered matter.

sources.note: Identity.Name=aider, Source variant FilesUnderCWD with filename .aider.chat.history.md, ExactName{Name: "aider"} process matcher.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
recipe.prompt: Reply with exactly the word: ok
verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new event for the .aider.chat.history.md within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to ready within 500 ms of birth
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event within 1000 ms
final session state is ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's two Expected bullets are (1) the session appears in `ready` within 1s of agent launch and (2) the PID is bound within 1s. Both are directly observable through irrlicht's emitted event stream for the claudecode adapter. The daemon's process scanner runs `pgrep -x claude` (per `ExactName{Name: "claude"}` in `core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/agent.go`), and on first detection it emits a `presession_created` row keyed by `proc-<PID>`. That pre-session is materialized in the `ready` state, so the spec's first bullet is satisfied independent of any transcript file existing on disk. The committed `claudecode/basic-turn/expected.jsonl` already anchors a `session_birth` phase (`expected_state: ready`) against recording start with `max_delay_ms: 1000`, confirming the timing budget is met in practice.

The second bullet — PID bound within 1s — is satisfied by the `pid_discovered` event emitted from `DiscoverPID` in `core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/pid.go`. Because the pre-session row IS keyed by PID from inception (via `processlifecycle.DiscoverPIDByCWD` matching `ProcessName="claude"`), PID binding happens at the same scanner tick as session birth. The fixture's `pid_bind` phase (`kind: pid_discovered`, `max_delay_ms: 1000`) corroborates this. Both spec bullets therefore land cleanly inside irrlicht's observability surface, anchored to agent launch — not to transcript materialization.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's OK)

Irrlicht does not see the UUID-keyed session row at launch, because claudecode uses lazy transcript materialization — the JSONL file under `~/.claude/projects/<projHash>/<UUID>.jsonl` is not written until the first user message. The spec's "session appears in ready within 1s" is nonetheless satisfied because the daemon's pre-session (`proc-<PID>`) row covers the idle window between launch and first user input. The eventual `transcript_new` event reconciles the pre-session into the UUID session once the transcript appears. This is the feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant pattern: the user-visible row exists on time even though the underlying file does not.
caveats: Claudecode uses lazy transcript materialization: the UUID-keyed session row only appears after the first user message lands. The spec's 'within 1s of agent launch' bullet is satisfied by the pre-session (`proc-<PID>`) row, not the UUID session — a known divergence between the user's mental model and the matrix verdict.

Both timing bullets are anchored to the daemon's process-scan tick, so a scanner interval longer than ~1s would violate the spec; the current configuration and basic-turn fixture confirm the budget is met in practice.

sources.note: Confirmed Identity.Name=claude-code, Source variant FilesUnderRoot with transcriptsDir() under ~/.claude/projects/, and ExactName{Name: "claude"} process matcher.
Confirmed DiscoverPID prefers ~/.claude/sessions/<UUID>.json metadata and falls back to DiscoverPIDByCWD matching ProcessName=claude — feeding the pid_discovered event.

Committed fixture asserts session_birth (expected_state: ready, max_delay_ms: 1000) and pid_bind (kind: pid_discovered, max_delay_ms: 1000) phases anchored to recording start — the same two bullets this scenario's spec asserts.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
no other claudecode session uses the same UUID
setup: Generate a fresh lowercased session UUID (run-cell.sh does this via uuidgen | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')
Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude prompts for trust on first encounter — the driver auto-accepts)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created event for proc-<PID> within 1000 ms of the tmux new-session timestamp
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to ready for proc-<PID> within 100 ms of presession_created (pre-session immediately in ready)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event for proc-<PID> within 1000 ms
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new event for the UUID session within 500 ms of the first send (claudecode creates the transcript on first input)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to ready for the UUID session within 500 ms of transcript_new
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event for the UUID session (PID re-bound to the UUID at handoff)
events.jsonl contains a presession_removed event for proc-<PID> at handoff
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 1 user line and 1 assistant line

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's two Expected bullets are (1) the session appears in `ready` within 1s of agent launch and (2) the PID is bound within 1s. Both are directly observable for the codex adapter. The daemon's process scanner emits a `presession_created` row keyed by `proc-<PID>` on first detection of the `codex` process, and codex eagerly creates its rollout transcript at `~/.codex/sessions/<YYYY>/<MM>/<DD>/rollout-<ts>-<UUID>.jsonl` on launch — so a `transcript_new` + `state_transition` → `ready` for the rollout-keyed session both land inside the daemon's first scanner tick (~30 ms in the captured recording).

PID binding happens at the same tick via `pid_discovered`. No transcript-materialization lag like claudecode has; the proc-<PID> pre-session row is a thin transitional cushion (~800 ms in the recording) until the rollout transcript supersedes it.

Both spec bullets therefore land cleanly inside irrlicht's observability surface, anchored to agent launch.
caveats: Codex's rollout file path encodes a UTC date directory (`YYYY/MM/DD/`), so the daemon's filesystem watcher must include that nested layout in its scan root — confirmed by FilesUnderRoot in core/adapters/inbound/agents/codex/agent.go.

sources.note: Identity.Name=codex, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.codex/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "codex"} process matcher.

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
recipe.prompt: Output exactly the two characters: ok
verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created or transcript_new event within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to ready within 500 ms of session birth
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event within 1000 ms
final session state is ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's two Expected bullets are (1) the session appears in `ready` within 1s of agent launch and (2) the PID is bound within 1s. Both are directly observable for the opencode adapter. Unlike file-tailing adapters, opencode uses a SQLite database (`~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db`) — the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and emits transcript_new + state_transition→ready for the UUID-keyed (`ses_…`) session.

In the recording, the `proc-<PID>` pre-session row is born at +72 ms (well under the 1000 ms budget) and pid_discovered fires at +74 ms; the SQLite-backed `ses_…` session reconciles at +528 ms when opencode's first part lands.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.
OpenCode shares its SQLite store across all opencode runs system-wide; per-cwd isolation only holds because the driver creates a fresh staging cwd per recording.

sources.note: Identity.Name=opencode, Source variant ProcessOwnedStore with PathForPID resolving to ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, ExactName{Name: "opencode"} process matcher.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated (run a single message in opencode TUI to confirm)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains an opencode session_created or transcript_new event within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to ready within 500 ms of session birth
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event within 1000 ms
final session state is ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's two Expected bullets are (1) the session appears in `ready` within 1s of agent launch and (2) the PID is bound within 1s. Both are directly observable for the pi adapter. The daemon's process scanner emits a `presession_created` row keyed by `proc-<PID>` for the `pi` process at first detection. Pi writes one JSONL session file per invocation under `~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<UUID>.jsonl` immediately on launch, so a `transcript_new` + `state_transition` → `ready` for the UUID-keyed session both land inside the daemon's first scanner tick.

PID binding happens at the same tick via `pid_discovered`. The recording confirms 18 events captured within the recording window, including the four required for spec compliance.
caveats: Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID — it generates one itself. The driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created transcript.

sources.note: Identity.Name=pi, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "pi"} process matcher.

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event within 1000 ms
